summary: This notebook implements an inference pipeline for the CZI Cryo-ET Object Identification competition, combining a 2D slice-based YOLO detector and a 3D volumetric UNet. It processes test zarr volumes by converting them to 8-bit, extracting patches, and running inference with test-time augmentation. Predictions from both models are spatially clustered using particle-specific radii to deduplicate and merge overlapping detections before generating the final submission. Readers should learn how to effectively blend 2D and 3D models for cryo-ET particle detection and handle spatial post-processing for accurate centroid estimation.
competition_flow: Loads pre-trained YOLO and 3D UNet models, processes test cryo-ET volumes via 2D slice inference and 3D patch-based inference with TTA, aggregates detections using spatial clustering, and generates a combined submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads test volumes from zarr files using zarr.open.
Accesses test runs and tomograms via the copick library.
data_processing: Converts volumes to 8-bit via percentile clipping and resizes 2D slices to 640x640 for YOLO.
Applies MONAI transforms (channel first, normalization, orientation) and extracts 96³ overlapping patches for the UNet.
Reconstructs patch predictions into full volumes and filters detections using 3D connected components with a blob size threshold.
ensembling: Blends YOLO and 3D UNet predictions, then applies particle-specific DBSCAN clustering to merge nearby detections and average their coordinates before saving the final submission.
insights: Blending 2D slice-based detection with 3D volumetric segmentation can improve robustness in cryo-ET particle identification.
Spatial deduplication using connected components and distance-based clustering is essential to avoid double-counting particles.
Test-time augmentation (flipping and rotating) effectively boosts 3D UNet inference performance without retraining.
